parts.label: a
parts.body: Find the value of the investment after 6 years.
parts.solution: **Plan:** compound interest with monthly compounding (booklet formula).$$FV = 5000\left(1 + \frac{4.2}{100 \times 12}\right)^{12 \times 6} = 5000(1.0035)^{72}$$$$= 6430.15 \text{ dollars (2 dp)}$$Using the GDC finance app or direct computation gives the same value.

parts.label: b
parts.body: Find the minimum number of whole years needed for the value of the investment to become greater than 10000 dollars.

markscheme.note: solve $5000(1.0035)^{12n} > 10000$, e.g. GDC table/graph or logarithms

parts.solution: **Plan:** the value doubles when $(1.0035)^{12n} = 2$.$$12n\,\ln(1.0035) = \ln 2 \Rightarrow n = \frac{\ln 2}{12\ln(1.0035)} \approx 16.5$$After 16 whole years the value is still below 10000 dollars; after 17 years it is above. The minimum number of whole years is $n = 17$.

questions.contextIntro: Bolor invests 5000 dollars in a savings account that pays an annual interest rate of 4.2%, compounded monthly. No further deposits or withdrawals are made.

parts.label: a
parts.body: Write down the number of fish when the lake was first stocked.

parts.label: b
parts.body: Find the number of fish after 5 years.
parts.solution: **Plan:** substitute $t = 5$.$$P(5) = 800\,e^{0.6} = 1457.70\ldots$$To 3 sf the model predicts $1460$ fish.

parts.label: c
parts.body: Find the value of $t$ when the population first reaches 2000.

markscheme.note: solve with the GDC (graph/solver) or logarithms: $t = \dfrac{\ln 2.5}{0.12}$

parts.solution: **Plan:** isolate the exponential, then take logarithms (or intersect the two graphs on the GDC).$$800\,e^{0.12t} = 2000 \Rightarrow e^{0.12t} = 2.5$$$$t = \frac{\ln 2.5}{0.12} = 7.63576\ldots \approx 7.64 \text{ years}$$

questions.contextIntro: The number of fish in a lake is modelled by $P(t) = 800\,e^{0.12t}$, where $t$ is the time in years after the lake was first stocked.

parts.label: a
parts.body: Find $BC$.
parts.solution: **Plan:** two sides and the included angle — cosine rule (booklet).$$BC^2 = 7.2^2 + 9.5^2 - 2(7.2)(9.5)\cos 38^{\circ} = 34.2901\ldots$$$$BC = 5.85578\ldots \approx 5.86 \text{ cm}$$

parts.label: b
parts.body: Find the area of triangle $ABC$.
parts.solution: **Plan:** the included angle is known, so use $\dfrac{1}{2}bc\sin A$.$$\text{area} = \frac{1}{2}(7.2)(9.5)\sin 38^{\circ} = 21.0556\ldots \approx 21.1 \text{ cm}^2$$

parts.label: a
parts.body: Find the length of the arc $AB$.
parts.solution: **Plan:** radian arc-length formula (booklet).$$l = r\theta = 6.4 \times 1.35 = 8.64 \text{ cm}$$(exact — no rounding needed).

parts.label: b
parts.body: Find the area of the sector $AOB$.
parts.solution: **Plan:** radian sector-area formula (booklet).$$A = \frac{1}{2}r^2\theta = \frac{1}{2}(40.96)(1.35) = 27.648 \approx 27.6 \text{ cm}^2$$

parts.label: c
parts.body: Find the area of the shaded region.
parts.solution: **Plan:** the segment is the sector minus the isosceles triangle $AOB$.$$\text{segment} = \frac{1}{2}r^2(\theta - \sin\theta) = \frac{1}{2}(40.96)(1.35 - \sin 1.35)$$$$= 27.648 - 19.9828\ldots = 7.66519\ldots \approx 7.67 \text{ cm}^2$$Remember: 1.35 is in **radians** — a GDC left in degree mode gives a wrong answer here.

questions.contextIntro: The diagram shows a circle with centre $O$ and radius 6.4 cm. Points $A$ and $B$ lie on the circle and $A\hat{O}B = 1.35$ radians. The region between the chord $[AB]$ and the arc $AB$ is shaded.

figure.alt_en: Circle with centre O and radius 6.4 cm; radii OA and OB make an angle of 1.35 radians; the segment between chord AB and the minor arc AB is shaded.

parts.label: a
parts.body: Write down $P(X < 160)$.

markscheme.note: $P(X < 160) = 0.5$ (the mean is the centre of symmetry)

parts.solution: The normal curve is symmetric about its mean, so exactly half the distribution lies below 160 g: $P(X < 160) = 0.5$.

parts.label: b
parts.body: Find $P(X < 148)$.
parts.solution: **Plan:** 148 g is exactly one standard deviation below the mean.$$z = \frac{148 - 160}{12} = -1$$$$P(X < 148) = P(Z < -1) = 0.1587\ldots \approx 0.159$$

parts.label: c

markscheme.note: GDC normal cdf between 150 and 175 (or standardise both bounds)

parts.solution: **Plan:** GDC normal cdf with lower 150, upper 175, $\mu = 160$, $\sigma = 12$.$$P(150 < X < 175) = 0.6920\ldots \approx 0.692$$

parts.label: d
parts.body: 20% of the apples have mass greater than $k$ grams. Find $k$.
parts.solution: **Plan:** inverse normal on the GDC with area 0.8.$$P(X < k) = 0.8 \Rightarrow z = 0.8416\ldots$$$$k = 160 + 12(0.8416\ldots) = 170.10\ldots \approx 170 \text{ g}$$

questions.contextIntro: The masses of apples from an orchard are normally distributed with mean 160 g and standard deviation 12 g. An apple is chosen at random. Let $X$ be its mass in grams, so $X \sim N(160,\ 12^2)$.

parts.label: b
parts.body: Find the sum of the first 10 terms.
parts.solution: **Plan:** geometric series formula (booklet).$$S_{10} = \frac{200(1.1^{10} - 1)}{0.1} = 3187.48\ldots \approx 3190$$

parts.label: c
parts.body: Find the least value of $n$ such that $S_n > 5000$.

markscheme.note: solve with logarithms or a GDC table: $n > \dfrac{\ln 3.5}{\ln 1.1} \approx 13.1$

parts.solution: **Plan:** solve the inequality for $n$, then round UP to a whole term.$$2000(1.1^{n} - 1) > 5000 \Rightarrow 1.1^{n} > 3.5$$$$n > \frac{\ln 3.5}{\ln 1.1} = 13.14\ldots$$A GDC table confirms $S_{13} \approx 4905 < 5000$ and $S_{14} \approx 5595 > 5000$. The least value is $n = 14$.

questions.contextIntro: A geometric sequence has first term $u_1 = 200$ and common ratio $r = 1.1$.

parts.label: a
parts.body: Write down the height of the platform.
parts.solution: The launch height is the value at $t = 0$: $h(0) = 1.2$ m.

parts.label: b
parts.body: Find the maximum height of the ball and the time at which it occurs.
parts.solution: **Plan:** the vertex of the parabola (GDC maximum, or $t = -b/2a$).$$t = \frac{-18}{2(-4.9)} = 1.8367\ldots \approx 1.84 \text{ s}$$$$h(1.8367\ldots) = 17.731\ldots \approx 17.7 \text{ m}$$

parts.label: c
parts.body: Find the time at which the ball hits the ground.

markscheme.note: solve $h(t) = 0$ with the GDC (or quadratic formula)
the negative root $t \approx -0.0655$ is rejected (time cannot be negative)

parts.solution: **Plan:** the ball lands when $h(t) = 0$.$$-4.9t^2 + 18t + 1.2 = 0$$The GDC gives $t = -0.0655$ or $t = 3.7390\ldots$. Time is non-negative, so the ball hits the ground at $t \approx 3.74$ s.

parts.label: d
parts.body: Find the length of time for which the ball is at least 10 m above the ground.

markscheme.note: length of time = difference of the roots

parts.solution: **Plan:** the ball is at least 10 m up between the two solutions of $h(t) = 10$.$$-4.9t^2 + 18t + 1.2 = 10 \Rightarrow -4.9t^2 + 18t - 8.8 = 0$$GDC: $t = 0.58068\ldots$ and $t = 3.0928\ldots$$$\Delta t = 3.0928 - 0.58068 = 2.5121\ldots \approx 2.51 \text{ s}$$

parts.label: e
parts.body: Write down the domain of the model.
parts.solution: The model applies from launch until the ball lands, so $0 \le t \le 3.74$ (using the landing time from part (c)).

questions.contextIntro: A ball is launched upwards from a platform. Its height above the ground is modelled by $h(t) = -4.9t^2 + 18t + 1.2$, where $h$ is in metres and $t \ge 0$ is the time in seconds after launch.

parts.label: a
parts.body: Find the expected number of successful throws.
parts.solution: **Plan:** binomial mean $E(X) = np$ (booklet).$$E(X) = 12 \times 0.6 = 7.2$$

parts.label: b
parts.body: Find $P(X = 7)$.
parts.solution: **Plan:** binomial probability with the GDC (binompdf).$$P(X = 7) = \binom{12}{7}(0.6)^7(0.4)^5 = 0.2270\ldots \approx 0.227$$

parts.label: c
parts.body: Find $P(X \ge 9)$.
parts.solution: **Plan:** complement with the cumulative binomial.$$P(X \ge 9) = 1 - P(X \le 8) = 1 - 0.7747\ldots = 0.2253\ldots \approx 0.225$$

parts.label: d
parts.body: Given that Sarnai makes at least 7 successful throws, find the probability that she makes at least 9.
parts.solution: **Plan:** the event $X \ge 9$ is inside $X \ge 7$, so$$P(X \ge 9 \mid X \ge 7) = \frac{P(X \ge 9)}{P(X \ge 7)} = \frac{0.2253\ldots}{0.6652\ldots} = 0.3387\ldots \approx 0.339$$
verify: Abs(Sum(binomial(12, k)*Rational(3, 5)**k*Rational(2, 5)**(12 - k), (k, 9, 12)).doit() / Sum(binomial(12, k)*Rational(3, 5)**k*Rational(2, 5)**(12 - k), (k, 7, 12)).doit() - 0.339) < 0.00051

parts.label: e
parts.body: Sarnai plays three such games, independently. Find the probability that she makes at least 9 successful throws in exactly two of the three games.

markscheme.note: let $Y$ = number of games with at least 9 throws: $Y \sim B(3,\ 0.2253\ldots)$

parts.solution: **Plan:** each game is a fresh trial whose success probability is the answer to part (c).$$q = P(X \ge 9) = 0.2253\ldots$$$$P(Y = 2) = \binom{3}{2}q^2(1 - q) = 3(0.2253\ldots)^2(1 - 0.2253\ldots) = 0.1180\ldots \approx 0.118$$Using the ROUNDED 0.225 here instead of the full GDC value changes the third significant figure — keep the unrounded value in the calculator.
verify: Abs(3*(Sum(binomial(12, k)*Rational(3, 5)**k*Rational(2, 5)**(12 - k), (k, 9, 12)).doit())**2*(1 - Sum(binomial(12, k)*Rational(3, 5)**k*Rational(2, 5)**(12 - k), (k, 9, 12)).doit()) - 0.118) < 0.00051

questions.contextIntro: Sarnai is a basketball player. Each free throw she attempts is successful with probability 0.6, independently of all other attempts. In one training game she attempts 12 free throws. Let $X$ be the number of successful throws, so $X \sim B(12,\ 0.6)$.

parts.label: a
parts.body: Write down $f(0)$.

parts.label: b
parts.solution: **Plan:** product rule with $u = x$, $v = e^{-0.4x}$.$$f'(x) = 1 \cdot e^{-0.4x} + x \cdot (-0.4)e^{-0.4x}$$$$= e^{-0.4x}(1 - 0.4x)$$as required.

parts.label: c
parts.body: Find the coordinates of the maximum point of the graph.
parts.solution: **Plan:** use the derivative from part (b).$$f'(x) = (1 - 0.4x)e^{-0.4x} = 0 \Rightarrow x = 2.5$$$$f(2.5) = 2.5\,e^{-1} = 0.91970\ldots \approx 0.920$$The maximum point is $(2.5,\ 0.920)$.

parts.label: d
parts.body: Find the $x$-coordinate of the point of inflexion of the graph.
parts.solution: **Plan:** the inflexion is where $f''$ changes sign.$$f''(x) = -0.4e^{-0.4x} + (1 - 0.4x)(-0.4)e^{-0.4x} = e^{-0.4x}(0.16x - 0.8)$$$$f''(x) = 0 \Rightarrow 0.16x = 0.8 \Rightarrow x = 5$$($f''$ changes from negative to positive there, confirming an inflexion).

parts.label: e
parts.body: Find the area of the shaded region.

markscheme.note: GDC numerical integral set up correctly

parts.solution: **Plan:** on this GDC paper, evaluate $\displaystyle\int_0^5 x\,e^{-0.4x}\,dx$ numerically.$$\int_0^5 x\,e^{-0.4x}\,dx = 3.7125\ldots \approx 3.71$$(The exact value, via integration by parts, is $\dfrac{25}{4} - \dfrac{75}{4}e^{-2}$ — a good check.)

questions.contextIntro: Let $f(x) = x\,e^{-0.4x}$ for $x \ge 0$. The diagram shows the graph of $y = f(x)$, and the shaded region is enclosed by the curve, the $x$-axis and the line $x = 5$.

figure.alt_en: Curve y = x times e to the minus 0.4 x rising from the origin to a single maximum then decaying; the region under the curve from x = 0 to the dashed line x = 5 is shaded.
